claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 14 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
circumstances: Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
To check your eligibility for Child Benefit, I'll need to ask you some questions about your situation.

**Let's start with the child you're claiming for:**

How old is the child? Please let me know if they are:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Also, could you tell me the child's name so I can keep track during this assessment?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hello! I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit and wanted to check if I'm eligible.

I actually have two children I'd like to ask about. My first child, Alex, is 14 years old. And I also have Blake, who's 19.

Should I talk about them one at a time, or would you like to know about both?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing those details! It's helpful to know about both children upfront. Let me assess eligibility for both **Alex (14 years old)** and **Blake (19 years old)**.

Let's start by working through the questions for each child. Since they're different ages, the requirements may differ.

---

**Starting with Alex (age 14):**

Since Alex is under 16, they automatically meet the age requirement. Now I need to check one more thing:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question for Alex:**

Is Alex working **less than 24 hours per week** (or not in paid work at all)?

*(This is usually straightforward for a 14-year-old, but I need to confirm.)*

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all - just school for now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that's what I expected for a 14-year-old! Alex is not in paid work, so that's fine. Let me continue with the next check for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question for Alex:**

Is Alex **NOT receiving any of the following benefits in their own right**?
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit

*(Most 14-year-olds wouldn't be receiving these, but I need to confirm.)*

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't get any benefits like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you. Let me continue to the next check for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question for Alex:**

Is Alex currently on an apprenticeship?

- If **no** (not on any apprenticeship) - that's fine
- If **yes**, the type matters:
  - Most apprenticeships in England and Scotland would disqualify
  - Foundation Apprenticeships in Wales are approved

*(Given Alex is 14 and in school, I expect this is a "no", but just confirming!)*

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't doing an apprenticeship - just regular school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's what I thought. Alex is doing well so far! Let me continue to check your (the claimant's) situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status. You can qualify through **any one** of the following:

**Do any of these apply to you?**

1. **Normally live in the UK** - You're a UK resident
2. **Settled status** - Under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **Pre-settled status** - With certain conditions (working, seeking work, studying, or have sufficient resources)
4. **Crown servant abroad** - Living abroad as a Crown servant

The most common is simply that you **normally live in the UK**. Does this apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, living in Manchester means you meet the UK residency requirement. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question - about your responsibility for Alex:**

Are you responsible for Alex? You can qualify if **any** of the following apply:

1. **You live with Alex** - The child lives in your home
2. **You contribute to Alex's upkeep** - You pay for food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc. (at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount)
3. **You pay maintenance** - Under a court order or agreement

Does any of these apply to you for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't live with me - they live with my ex-partner.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand. Since Alex doesn't live with you, let me ask a follow-up question:

**Do you contribute to Alex's upkeep?**

This could include paying for things like:
- Food
- Clothes
- Pocket money
- Housing costs
- Presents

Or do you pay maintenance for Alex under a court order or agreement?

The contribution needs to be at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount (which is around £25.60 per week for an eldest/only child, or £16.95 for additional children).

Do you contribute financially to Alex's care in any of these ways?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not currently contributing financially for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for being honest about that. Unfortunately, this means you wouldn't be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I've noted that for **Alex**, since you don't live with them and aren't currently contributing financially to their care, you wouldn't meet the "responsibility" requirement for Child Benefit.

However, if you were to start contributing to Alex's upkeep (at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount), you could potentially become eligible in the future.

---

**Now let's move on to Blake (age 19):**

Let me start the assessment for your second child.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Since Blake is 19 years old (in the 16-19 age bracket), there are additional requirements around education or training.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question about Blake:**

For young people aged 16-19, Child Benefit eligibility depends on their education or training status.

Is Blake:
1. **Currently in education or training** (e.g., A-levels, college course, approved training)?
2. **Recently left education** (within the last 20 weeks)?
3. **Neither** - not in education/training and hasn't been recently?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake is currently in education - doing A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, Blake is doing A-levels. Let me check the education level requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
A-levels are on the approved qualifications list - that's good news! I just need to confirm a couple of things about Blake's studies:

1. **Is Blake studying full-time?** (This means at least 12 hours per week of supervised study)

2. **Is the course funded by an employer** or does Blake have an agreed future job with an employer linked to the course?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake is studying full-time at sixth form college. And no, it's not employer-funded - just the regular A-level course.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! A-levels at sixth form college, full-time and not employer-funded - that's approved education. Let me continue checking the requirements for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question for Blake:**

Is Blake working **less than 24 hours per week** in paid employment (or not working at all)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't work - just focuses on the A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's fine - Blake not working makes things straightforward. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question for Blake:**

Is Blake **NOT receiving** any of the following benefits in their own right?
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't get any benefits like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question for Blake:**

Is Blake on any kind of apprenticeship, or just doing the regular A-level course at sixth form?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, just the regular A-level course - no apprenticeship.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect. Now I need to check your situation as the claimant again (for Blake specifically).

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: You already confirmed you live in the UK (Manchester), so the residency requirement is met. Let me move to the responsibility check for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Important question for Blake:**

Are you responsible for Blake? Does **any** of the following apply?

1. **Blake lives with you** in Manchester
2. **You contribute to Blake's upkeep** (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. **You pay maintenance** for Blake under a court order or agreement

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake lives at home with me in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Blake lives with you, so you meet the responsibility requirement. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question for Blake:**

Is anyone else currently claiming (or planning to claim) Child Benefit for Blake?

- **No** - no one else is claiming
- **Yes, but we've agreed** - someone else was claiming but you've agreed that you should be the one to claim
- **Yes, and it's disputed** - there's a disagreement about who should claim

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, my ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and Blake lives with them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see - thank you for that clarification. This is important information. You mentioned earlier that Blake lives with you, but you've now said Blake lives with your ex-partner who is already claiming Child Benefit.

Could you clarify Blake's living situation for me?

1. **Does Blake live primarily with you** in Manchester?
2. **Does Blake live primarily with your ex-partner?**
3. **Does Blake split time between both homes?**

This will help me determine the correct eligibility outcome.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I apologize for the confusion - let me clarify. Blake does live with me here in Manchester. However, my ex-partner, who also lives with us, is the one currently claiming the Child Benefit for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Ah, thank you for clarifying! That makes sense now. So Blake lives in your household, your ex-partner also lives there, and your ex-partner is currently the one claiming Child Benefit for Blake.

In this case, only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time. Since your ex-partner is already claiming, I need to ask:

**Have you and your ex-partner agreed that you should take over the claim for Blake instead?**

- **Yes, we've agreed** - Your ex-partner is willing to stop their claim so you can claim instead
- **No / It's disputed** - You haven't agreed, or there's a disagreement about who should be the claimant

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, we haven't agreed on that - they're still claiming it.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Since there's already an existing claim by your ex-partner and you haven't agreed on transferring it to you, this situation would need to be resolved by HMRC.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I now have enough information to provide you with my assessment for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## Summary: Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment

### **Alex (14 years old)** ❌ **NOT ELIGIBLE**

**Reason:** You don't meet the "responsibility" requirement.

Alex doesn't live with you and you're not currently contributing financially to their care. To claim Child Benefit, you must either:
- Live with the child, OR
- Contribute at least the Child Benefit amount towards their upkeep

**What you could do:** If you started contributing financially to Alex's care (at least around £16.95-£25.60 per week towards food, clothes, housing, pocket money, etc.), you could potentially become eligible in the future.

---

### **Blake (19 years old)** ⏸️ **DEFERRED TO HMRC**

**Reason:** Your ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake.

You would otherwise meet all the eligibility criteria for Blake - they're in approved education (A-levels), living with you, and you meet the residency requirements. However, only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time, and your ex-partner already has an active claim.

**What you could do:**
1. **Talk to your ex-partner** - If you can agree that you should be the one to claim, they can stop their claim and you can apply
2. **Contact HMRC** - If you can't agree, you can both contact the Child Benefit Office. HMRC will consider:
   - Who the child lives with most of the time
   - Who contributes most to the child's upkeep
   - The child's best interests

**Useful link:** [Child Benefit when a child lives with someone else](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit-child-lives-with-someone-else)

---

Do you have any questions about this assessment or need guidance on next steps?

reasonings: Alex is 14 years old (under 16), which meets the age requirement. However, Alex does not live with you and you are not currently contributing financially to Alex's care. To be eligible for Child Benefit, you must be 'responsible' for the child, which means either living with them OR contributing at least the Child Benefit amount towards their upkeep (food, clothes, housing, pocket money, etc.). Since neither condition is met, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 19 years old and in approved education (full-time A-levels at sixth form college), which meets the qualifying child requirements. Blake doesn't work 24+ hours per week, doesn't receive ESA or Universal Credit, and isn't on a disqualifying apprenticeship. You live in the UK and Blake lives with you, so the residency and responsibility requirements are met. However, your ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and you haven't agreed on who should be the claimant. Since only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time, and there's no agreement to transfer the claim to you, this matter would need to be decided by HMRC.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are not currently eligible to claim Child Benefit for either child. For Alex, you don't meet the responsibility requirement as Alex doesn't live with you and you're not contributing to their upkeep. For Blake, while you would otherwise meet all the eligibility criteria, your ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and without an agreement between you, HMRC would need to make a decision on who should receive the benefit.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Payments cover the child's upkeep
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.guidance: You must be responsible for the child, which means either living with them OR paying at least the Child Benefit amount towards their care (food, clothes, housing, pocket money, etc.). This can include maintenance payments under court order.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.reason: Multiple people claim responsibility for the child and cannot agree who should claim Child Benefit
response.guidance: When two or more people are responsible for a child and cannot agree who should claim, HMRC will make the decision. HMRC considers: (1) Who the child lives with most of the time (primary residence), (2) Who contributes most to the child's upkeep, (3) The child's best interests, (4) Continuity of existing arrangements. Both parties should contact the Child Benefit Office with evidence of their circumstances. HMRC's decision is binding. Common scenarios: separated parents with shared custody, child living part-time with each parent, grandparent vs parent both contributing.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 14 years old (under 16), which meets the age requirement. However, Alex does not live with you and you are not currently contributing financially to Alex's care. To be eligible for Child Benefit, you must be 'responsible' for the child, which means either living with them OR contributing at least the Child Benefit amount towards their upkeep (food, clothes, housing, pocket money, etc.). Since neither condition is met, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 19 years old and in approved education (full-time A-levels at sixth form college), which meets the qualifying child requirements. Blake doesn't work 24+ hours per week, doesn't receive ESA or Universal Credit, and isn't on a disqualifying apprenticeship. You live in the UK and Blake lives with you, so the residency and responsibility requirements are met. However, your ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and you haven't agreed on who should be the claimant. Since only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time, and there's no agreement to transfer the claim to you, this matter would need to be decided by HMRC.

response.overall_reasoning: You are not currently eligible to claim Child Benefit for either child. For Alex, you don't meet the responsibility requirement as Alex doesn't live with you and you're not contributing to their upkeep. For Blake, while you would otherwise meet all the eligibility criteria, your ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and without an agreement between you, HMRC would need to make a decision on who should receive the benefit.
